# **Price Search Definition**

## General

The following is a description of a suggested approach and some details of a price-search mechanism for the app, added as a feature that follows the image-enhancer AI operation.

## **General Characteristics of the Service**

To compare the price of an item based on an image across specific websites, there isn't a single "price comparison API" you can just point an image at. Instead, you need to build a multi-step pipeline that combines multimodal understanding with real-time web search. The recommended approach is to use Gemini 3.1 Pro or Flash in combination with Google Search Grounding.

**The Recommended Workflow**

**Item Identification (Gemini Multimodal):** Use a Gemini model (like gemini-3.1-pro-preview) to analyze the image. You would prompt the model to identify the item, its brand, model number, or any unique identifiers visible in the image. Why: Gemini's multimodal capabilities are excellent at recognizing objects and extracting text (like brand names or model numbers) directly from images.

**Web Search (Google Search Grounding):** Take the item description/identifiers generated by Gemini and use the Google Search Grounding tool within the Gemini API. This allows the model to perform a real-time web search for that specific item. Why: This provides the model with up-to-date information from the web, rather than relying on its internal training data.

**Price Extraction & Comparison (Gemini):** Once the model has the search results (which will contain links and snippets from various shopping sites), you prompt the model to parse those results, identify the prices on those specific sites, and compare them. Why: Gemini is highly capable of structured data extraction (e.g., converting unstructured search snippets into a JSON object containing siteName, price, and currency).

**Why this is the best approach**

* Multimodal Reasoning: You need an AI that can "see" the image to know what to search for.

* Real-time Data: You need the Google Search tool to access current pricing, which changes constantly.

* Structured Output: You need the model to parse the messy, unstructured web search results into a clean, comparable format.

**Important Considerations & Challenges**

* Anti-Scraping: Many e-commerce sites have robust anti-scraping measures. Even with Google Search Grounding, some sites may block or limit the information the model can retrieve.

* Item Ambiguity: If an image is generic (e.g., "a white t-shirt"), the model will struggle to find a specific price match. The more specific the item (e.g., "Apple Magic Mouse"), the better the results will be.

* Dynamic Pricing: Prices on websites change frequently, and search snippets might be slightly outdated.

If you decide to implement this, you would start by using the @google/genai SDK to call ai.models.generateContent with the googleSearch tool enabled, passing the image as part of the contents parameter.

**The existing code is supposed to be an excellent starting point.**

We have already implemented the core logic for multimodal analysis (using Gemini to "see" the image and generate text). However, to make it effective for price comparison/search, you should refine the approach slightly.

**Why it's a great base:**

* **Multimodal Infrastructure:** You already have the code to send an image to Gemini and parse the JSON response.

* **Prompting Logic:** You already have a structured prompt setup.

**What needs to change (The "Search-Optimized" approach):**

The current prompt is optimized for marketing (e.g., "write a title for an eCommerce site"). For price comparison, you need the model to act as a product identifier.

### 1\. Refine the Prompt

Instead of asking for a marketing title/description, you should ask Gemini to extract searchable product identifiers.

*Current (Marketing-focused):* "כתוב כותרת קצרה... ותיאור קצר... עבור רשומה באתר מסחר אלקטרוני..."

*Recommended (Search-focused):* "Analyze this image and identify the exact product. Extract the following in JSON format: 'brand', 'modelName', 'modelNumber' (if visible), and a 'searchQuery' string optimized for Google Shopping (e.g., 'Brand Model Name SKU')."

### 2\. Integrate the Search Tool

Instead of just generating text, you will add the googleSearch tool to the Gemini API call.

```ts
// Example of the refined API call
const response = await ai.models.generateContent({
  model: 'gemini-3-flash-preview',
  contents: {
    parts: [
      { inlineData: { data: base64Data, mimeType: mimeType } },
      { text: 'Identify this product and provide a search query for price comparison.' }
    ],
  },
  config: {
    tools: [{ googleSearch: {} }], // <--- ADD THIS
    responseMimeType: "application/json",
  },
});
```

## **Summary of the Workflow**

* **Keep the Image Enhancement:** Your current gemini-3.1-flash-image-preview call is great for making the image look good for your UI. Keep that.

* **Add a "Search" Step:** Create a new function (or expand the current one) that calls Gemini with the googleSearch tool, using a prompt optimized for identifying the product.

* **Parse and Display:** Take the search results (URLs and snippets) and display them in your UI.

Here is a screenshot of the button to be added:  
![][image1]

### 3\. Target Specific Retailers

You can restrict the search to specific websites by using the `site:` operator in your search query. This is a very effective way to get highly relevant results from the specific Hebrew retailers you care about.

**How it works:** When you construct the search query to send to Gemini, you can format it like this:

`"Product Name" (site:site1.co.il OR site:site2.co.il OR site:site3.co.il)`

**Example Implementation:** When you call the Gemini API, you would dynamically build the query string based on your list of target sites:

```ts
const targetSites = ['site:zap.co.il', 'site:ksp.co.il', 'site:ivory.co.il'];
const siteQuery = targetSites.join(' OR ');
const searchQuery = `"${productName}" (${siteQuery})`;
```

Here is the list:

[https://yad2.co.il](https://yad2.co.il)

[https://ksp.co.il](https://ksp.co.il)

[https://shop.super-pharm.co.il/](https://shop.super-pharm.co.il/)

[https://alm.co.il](https://alm.co.il)

[https://www.payngo.co.il/](https://www.payngo.co.il/)

[https://www.ikea.com/il/he/](https://www.ikea.com/il/he/)

[https://www.officedepot.co.il/](https://www.officedepot.co.il/)

[https://www.homecenter.co.il/](https://www.homecenter.co.il/)

Note: Let have the list as an external JSON file that can be edited later to remove /add sites without coding.

The UX  
1\. User activates the "AI Enhacement" button (stars) too improve image and get definition \- already with text ready for search price as well as marketing. The marketing text goes to the defined fields (already defined) and the search-oriented text ready for price comparison. 

2\. On the same line of the price input box, add at the left side a button similar to the image enhancer button. 

3\. The user can simply enter a price or click this button and activate the price search. If the price search gets several pricing matches, the text in the price box is formatted as a range: "100-150 ש״ח"

4\. While searching, show a popup with cycling “operating” icon, and names of sites being accessed. Popup disappears when search ends. If no match found, show “no match” text in the price box ( Hebrew: לא נמצא )
